item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's no math error.

When you imagine the imaginary dollar, you're making it -1.
Imagining the imaginary dollar. Imaginary squared.

Get it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It clearly implied cancelling in the interview log, but I get the imagining an imaginary dollar yielding i*i effect now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't cancel it out the dollar, it converts the regular dollar to into an imaginary dollar- $$i$. The one you're imagining, to be specific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea and the writing, but I can't help but notice the math error. The coin has a denomination of i, not -1. The number i is not a negative number, it's a number on an entirely different axis that doesn't exist in the "real" world. So there's no logical reason why it would cancel out 1 dollar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Imagines -1million i-dollors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
goddammit

(upvote)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked and upvoted this. I think the interview is worth it for clarifying how the "math" works; it's a little goofy (ha!) and breaks suspension of disbelief, but added more than it detracted for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Please note that this coin has no real monetary value.

Ha!

item.title: Re: Figured it out (spoilers)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers
Hi hello welcome to collapsible please enjoy your stay

Welp, turns out I've been using the wrong castle. I wrote it as "Cinderella Castle," but apparently the modern logo takes influence from both castles, while the pre-2006 version was only based on Sleeping Beauty. Thanks for the heads-up. Time for a(n incredibly trivial) revision!

Also, now I can add "history of Walt Disney Pictures logo" to "history of the US minimum wage" and "US Mint press releases" to my Great Big List of Obscure Things I Researched While Writing This.

item.title: Figured it out (spoilers)
item.content:encoded:
I found out who exactly the coin was intended to commemorate. According to the press release, the coin was to begin production on December 5, 2001, and was intended to commemorate the 100 year anniversary of a famous figure's birth. Based on the number of blacked-out letters in the figure's name within the press release, and on me looking at a list of people born on December 5th (and specifically the year 1901), I figured out that it was none other than…

+ Spoiler warning!
- Thank you for looking at spoilers.

Walt Disney.

+ News article but with all the infohazards completely uncensored! MAJOR SPOILER ALERT!
– hide block

WASHINGTON — On December 5, 2001, the United States Mint will celebrate the 100th birthday of Walt Disney with the release of the American Imagination Commemorative Silver Dollar (product code 01CE). For generations, Disney's work has captured the imaginations of Americans young and old, and we honor his memory with this coin's appropriately whimsical denomination of $i — the basis of imaginary numbers.

The obverse side of the American Imagination Commemorative Silver Dollar features a portrait of Walt Disney beside the iconic Mickey Mouse logo. Inscriptions include include "LIBERTY," "IN GOD WE TRUST," "2001," and "WALT DISNEY."

The reverse side features the Sleeping Beauty castle, as seen in the Walt Disney Pictures logo. Inscriptions include "E PLURIBUS UNUM," "UNITED STATES OF AMERICA," "i DOLLARS," and "If you can dream it, you can do it."

Please note that this coin has no real monetary value.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for a pretty novel idea that was executed well.

I'm torn on the interview, personally. I somewhat agree with other posters that the conclusions drawn are absurd, but the way it ends is pretty funny and I have to admit I like that a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this up until the interview, which honestly kind of killed it for me. All of the conclusions the coin artist comes to are so utterly out there as to be unbelievable, for someone who isn't aware of anomalous stuff. Almost pushed me to downvote, but I'll leave it at a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I expected some people to figure out the person on the coin, but I'm very pleased you caught the meaning behind Gresham (assuming you're referring to what I think you're referring to).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this. The interview was worth it just for the employee's name.

Figuring out who the commemorative coin was supposed to commemorate - hah!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They don't remember.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My 2c:

  • I'm also getting the Math Processing Error. It's probably better fixed, but it's kinda fitting at the moment.
  • I know a bit about i, but thought the interview was helpful to make the "mental multiplication" point, which I hadn't really gathered up until then.
  • I am tempted to second the call for an exploration of the time value of the debt, which I agree was a highlight of the article, but I wonder whether spelling it out would remove some of the mystery of it and undermine its effectiveness as a tool of wonder.

Upvoted for a terrific (and highly usable) concept, executed punchily in a way that doesn't outstay its welcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pun is strong with this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I concur the interview is unnecessary. The most interesting part of this article is left unexplored—what are those without a checking account or currency in their proximity doing to work off the debt? An interview/exploration log on that topic rather than what you already have could be quite compelling.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh.
My.
God.

Needs to be done nowish, relevart!

Edit: my only issue was I found the explanation in the interview wholly unnecessary. I guess some people might need it though?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He he, I like it.

EDIT: Should've made the coin out of antimony though. Antimony antimony

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Come to think of it, that's a good way to explain $i$ in lay terms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The insurgency would really like to steal that and put it on world wide television or just youtubes recommended page.
Get this viral, economic collapse and XK in about one week.

"Today on the news, crisis continues as more and more money leaves the economy do to a bug linked to this picture."

Little, devastating and a potential emergency teleporter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's supposed to say "i dollars," but I used the curly i in LaTeX (like this: $i$) to make it stand out better. It works on my phone and my laptop, but I guess the formatting doesn't work everywhere else. I'll try to contact some of the staff's tech gurus to sort this out. Thanks for bringing this up, and I'm sorry it didn't work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm getting a "Math Processing Error" dollars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Second SCP! Thanks to Aiden Eldritch, Modern_Erasmus, GerrymanderBassist, and Meta and NoxRoxSox in chat for looking this thing over.
